perturbation.original_text: “Proprietary Information” means confidential, non -public or other proprietary in formation including, without limitation, trade secrets, technical information, including algorithms, code, data, designs, documentation, drawings, formulae, hardware, software, know -how, ideas, inventions, whether patentable or not, photographs, recordings , procedures, processes, reports, research, samples, sketches, specifications, operations, plans, products, financial information, including pricing and other proprietary information that is disclosed under the terms of this Agreement by Disclosing Party o r its Affiliates to the Recipient. Proprietary Information shall be marked as proprietary, confidential or with another suitable legend at the time of disclosure or otherwise designated in writing as proprietary by the Disclosing Party within thirty (30) days of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: “Proprietary Information” means information disclosed under this agreement. Proprietary Information shall be marked as proprietary, confidential or with another suitable legend at the time of disclosure or otherwise designated in writing as proprietary by the Disclosing Party within thirty (30) days of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the detailed list of what constitutes 'Proprietary Information' (trade secrets, technical information, etc.), the modified text creates an in-text contradiction. Later sections rely on the specific definition to determine the scope of confidentiality and non-disclosure obligations. The removal makes it unclear which types of information are actually protected and enforceable under the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Curtiss -Wright and Company agree that, as a condition to the receipt of Proprietary Information hereunder, Recipient shall: (i) not disclose, directly or indire ctly, to any third party any portion of the Proprietary Information without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party (except to its Affiliates, and its or their Authorized Parties who have a need to know the Proprietary Information and only to the extent necessary for the Purpose); (ii) not use the Proprietary Information except for the Purpose; (iii) promptly return or destroy, at the Disclosing Party’s request, all materials and documentation containing the Proprietary Information received hereun der except for copies of any computer records or electronic files containing the Proprietary Information, which have been created by automated processes such as archiving or back -up procedures; (iv) take all reasonably necessary precautions to protect the confidentiality of the Proprietary Information received hereunder and exercise at least the same degree of care in safeguarding the Proprietary Information as Recipient, its Affiliates, or its or their Authorized Parties would with its own proprietary info rmation; (v) inform all Authorized Parties of Recipient and its Affiliates to whom Proprietary Information may be disclosed or made available of the proprietary interest of the Disclosing Party therein and of the obligations of Recipient with respect there to; and (vi) promptly advise the Disclosing Party in writing upon learning of any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text provides a detailed definition of "Proprietary Information," which includes trade secrets, technical information, algorithms, and other specific types of data. The changed text significantly narrows this definition to simply "information disclosed under this agreement." This omission of the detailed list creates a direct contradiction with the contradicted text, which refers to "Proprietary Information" without the detailed context. The contradicted text obligates the recipient to protect 'Proprietary Information', and without the original definition, it becomes unclear what information falls under this obligation, thus hindering enforceability and creating a strong in-text contradiction as per the definition of Omissions - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Proprietary Information shall not include information which: (i) is or becomes a part of the public domain without any action by, or involvement of, Recipient, its Affiliates, or its or their Authorized Parties; (ii) is received by Rec ipient, its Affiliates, or its or their Authorized Parties from a third party without a duty of confidentiality owed to the Disclosing Party; (iii) is independently developed by Recipient, its Affiliates, or its or their Authorized Parties without use of or without reference to the Proprietary Information; (iv) is already in the possession of Recipient, its Affiliates, or its or their Authorized Parties and Recipient, its Affiliates, or its or their Authorized Parties can demonstrate with written proof that the information was in the possession of Recipient, its Affiliates, or its or their Authorized Parties prior to the disclosure of the Proprietary Information by the Disclosing Party; or (v) is disclosed by Recipient, its Affiliates, or its or their Author ized Parties with the prior written approval of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Proprietary Information shall not include information which: (i) is or becomes a part of the public domain without any action by, or involvement of, Recipient, its Affiliates, or its or their Authorized Parties; or (v) is disclosed by Recipient, its Affiliates, or its or their Author ized Parties with the prior written approval of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing exceptions (ii), (iii) and (iv) in the definition, it creates an in-text contradiction with later parts of the agreement that may assume these scenarios are not considered Proprietary Information. This introduces ambiguity on what exactly is protected under this NDA.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Curtiss -Wright and Company agree that Prop rietary Information may be disclosed under this Agreement with appropriate restrictive legends provided thereon to the U.S. Government under the provisions of FAR, or similar regulations of other Government agencies limiting use and disclosure, but only to the extent such limited use or disclosure is specifically related to the Purpose. If either party or any of their respective Affiliates or Authorized Parties is requested or required, by interrogatories, subpoena, or similar legal process, to disclose an y Proprietary Information, such party agrees to provide the Disclosing Party with prompt written notice of each such request, to the extent practicable, so that the Disclosing Party may seek an appropriate protective order, waive compliance by the Recipien t with the provisions of this Agreement, or both. If, absent the entry of a protective order or receipt of a waiver, the Recipient is, in the opinion of its counsel, legally compelled to disclose such Proprietary Information, the Recipient may disclose su ch Proprietary Information to the persons and to the extent required without liability under this Agreement and will use best efforts to obtain confidential treatment for any Proprietary Information so disclosed.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits exceptions to the definition of Proprietary Information, specifically (ii), (iii), and (iv). The explanation states that this creates a contradiction because later parts of the agreement, such as the contradicted text concerning disclosures under FAR regulations and legal processes, may assume that these scenarios are not considered Proprietary Information. By removing these exceptions, the scope of 'Proprietary Information' broadens, potentially conflicting with the provisions in the contradicted text that allow for disclosure under specific circumstances.

perturbation.original_text: 2.1. Curtiss -Wright and Company agree that, as a condition to the receipt of Proprietary Information hereunder, Recipient shall: (i) not disclose, directly or indire ctly, to any third party any portion of the Proprietary Information without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party (except to its Affiliates, and its or their Authorized Parties who have a need to know the Proprietary Information and only to the extent necessary for the Purpose); (ii) not use the Proprietary Information except for the Purpose; (iii) promptly return or destroy, at the Disclosing Party’s request, all materials and documentation containing the Proprietary Information received hereun der except for copies of any computer records or electronic files containing the Proprietary Information, which have been created by automated processes such as archiving or back -up procedures; (iv) take all reasonably necessary precautions to protect the confidentiality of the Proprietary Information received hereunder and exercise at least the same degree of care in safeguarding the Proprietary Information as Recipient, its Affiliates, or its or their Authorized Parties would with its own proprietary info rmation; (v) inform all Authorized Parties of Recipient and its Affiliates to whom Proprietary Information may be disclosed or made available of the proprietary interest of the Disclosing Party therein and of the obligations of Recipient with respect there to; and (vi) promptly advise the Disclosing Party in writing upon learning of any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.1. Curtiss -Wright and Company agree that, as a condition to the receipt of Proprietary Information hereunder, Recipient shall: (i) not disclose, directly or indire ctly, to any third party any portion of the Proprietary Information without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party (except to its Affiliates, and its or their Authorized Parties who have a need to know the Proprietary Information and only to the extent necessary for the Purpose); (ii) not use the Proprietary Information except for the Purpose; (iii) promptly return or destroy, at the Disclosing Party’s request, all materials and documentation containing the Proprietary Information received hereun der except for copies of any computer records or electronic files containing the Proprietary Information;
perturbation.explanation: By removing clauses (iv), (v), and (vi), it creates an in-text contradiction with later sections. Specifically section 2.2 requires the receiving party to advise authorized parties of the proprietary nature of the information, this contradicts the removal of clause (v) which directly stipulated this agreement. Now, the agreement no longer explicitly dictates that recipients must inform authorized parties of proprietary interests or obligations, creating ambiguity and potential unenforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.2. The Recipient further agrees that prior to disclosing any Proprietary Information to its Affiliates, or its or their Authorized Parties, as allowed hereunder, the Receiving Party shall advise such Affiliates and/or Authorized Parties of the proprietary n ature of the Proprietary Information, and either: (x) direct them to abide by the terms of this Agreement; or (y) ensure that they are under written agreement with the Recipient that establishes non -disclosure and limited use restrictions regarding the Pro prietary Information that are no less restrictive than those set forth herein. The Recipient agrees to be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by its Affiliates and its or their Authorized Parties.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits clauses (iv), (v), and (vi) from the original text. This directly contradicts section 2.2, which requires the receiving party to advise authorized parties of the proprietary nature of the information. The removal of clause (v), which explicitly stated this agreement, creates a strong in-text contradiction, as the agreement no longer explicitly dictates that recipients must inform authorized parties of proprietary interests or obligations, causing ambiguity and potential unenforceability. This aligns with the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 2.2. The Recipient further agrees that prior to disclosing any Proprietary Information to its Affiliates, or its or their Authorized Parties, as allowed hereunder, the Receiving Party shall advise such Affiliates and/or Authorized Parties of the proprietary n ature of the Proprietary Information, and either: (x) direct them to abide by the terms of this Agreement; or (y) ensure that they are under written agreement with the Recipient that establishes non -disclosure and limited use restrictions regarding the Pro prietary Information that are no less restrictive than those set forth herein. The Recipient agrees to be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by its Affiliates and its or their Authorized Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2. The Recipient agrees to be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by its Affiliates and its or their Authorized Parties.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement that the Recipient advise Affiliates/Authorized Parties of the proprietary nature of the information, it introduces an in-text contradiction with section 2.1. The recipient is held responsible for breaches by Affiliates/Authorized Parties, however, now there is no mechanism in place to notify the parties that the agreement is in place, thus making it difficult to make the recipient responsible.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1. Curtiss -Wright and Company agree that, as a condition to the receipt of Proprietary Information hereunder, Recipient shall: (i) not disclose, directly or indire ctly, to any third party any portion of the Proprietary Information without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party (except to its Affiliates, and its or their Authorized Parties who have a need to know the Proprietary Information and only to the extent necessary for the Purpose); (ii) not use the Proprietary Information except for the Purpose; (iii) promptly return or destroy, at the Disclosing Party’s request, all materials and documentation containing the Proprietary Information received hereun der except for copies of any computer records or electronic files containing the Proprietary Information;
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the Recipient to advise Affiliates/Authorized Parties of the proprietary nature of the information. The changed text removes this requirement, and the explanation states that this creates an in-text contradiction with section 2.1, as there is now no mechanism in place to notify these parties of the agreement. Section 2.1 outlines the requirements for receipt of Proprietary Information. Without the notification requirement from the original text (now removed), section 2.1 is contradicted because it assumes parties receiving the information are aware of their obligations, which is no longer guaranteed. This is a strong contradiction because it directly undermines the conditions for receiving proprietary information as stated in 2.1.

perturbation.original_text: 2.4. This Agreement shall be effective as of the Effective Date below and shall expire three (3) years after the Effective Date. Either party may terminate this Agreement without cause by giving thirty (30) days prior written notice of termination to the other party. The obligation of confidentia lity and non -disclosure with respect to Proprietary Information shall expire on the later of the date: (i) that is five (5) years from the Effective Date below; or (ii) when the Proprietary Information is no longer protected as a trade secret under applica ble law.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.4. This Agreement shall be effective as of the Effective Date below and shall expire three (3) years after the Effective Date. Either party may terminate this Agreement without cause by giving thirty (30) days prior written notice of termination to the other party. The obligation of confidentia lity and non -disclosure with respect to Proprietary Information shall expire on the later of the date: (i) that is five (5) years from the Effective Date below;
perturbation.explanation: By removing (ii) 'when the Proprietary Information is no longer protected as a trade secret under applicable law.', it could create an in-text contradiction with the definition of proprietary information. The agreement now stipulates that confidentiality lasts five years regardless of trade secret status. If information ceases to be a trade secret before the 5 year mark, it creates a contradictory situation because earlier it was defined as proprietary information and now it is no longer a trade secret under applicable law, which would have led to a different result.
perturbation.contradicted_text: “Proprietary Information” means information disclosed under this agreement. Proprietary Information shall be marked as proprietary, confidential or with another suitable legend at the time of disclosure or otherwise designated in writing as proprietary by the Disclosing Party within thirty (30) days of disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the condition for confidentiality expiring when information is no longer a trade secret. This directly contradicts the definition of "Proprietary Information," which implies that information only qualifies as such if it's treated and designated as proprietary, potentially including trade secret status. If information ceases to be a trade secret before the 5-year mark, the changed text enforces confidentiality despite the 'Proprietary Information' definition no longer applying, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3. EXPORT CONTROL Recipient agrees that it will not export, re -export, resell or transfer, or otherwise ship or deliver any Proprietary Information, product, assembly, component or any technical data or software which violates any export control s or limitations imposed by the United States or any other governmental authority, or to any country for which an export license or other governmental approval is required at the time of export without first obtaining all necessary licenses or other approv als. The Recipient shall indemnify and hold the Disclosing Party harmless from all claims, demands, damages, costs, fines, penalties, attorneys’ fees and all other expenses arising from the failure of the Recipient to comply with this clause and any local , state, provincial, or federal regulations governing the transfer of controlled information or data.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. EXPORT CONTROL Recipient agrees that it will not export, re -export, resell or transfer, or otherwise ship or deliver any product which violates any export control s or limitations imposed by the United States or any other governmental authority, or to any country for which an export license or other governmental approval is required at the time of export without first obtaining all necessary licenses or other approv als. The Recipient shall indemnify and hold the Disclosing Party harmless from all claims, demands, damages, costs, fines, penalties, attorneys’ fees and all other expenses arising from the failure of the Recipient to comply with this clause and any local , state, provincial, or federal regulations governing the transfer of controlled information or data.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'Proprietary Information, assembly, component or any technical data or software' it creates an in-text contradiction with section 1, the DEFINITIONS section, which specifies what Proprietary Information refers to. The current state of the agreement does not properly regulate export of 'Proprietary Information, assembly, component or any technical data or software', this introduces ambiguity in enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: “Proprietary Information” means information disclosed under this agreement. Proprietary Information shall be marked as proprietary, confidential or with another suitable legend at the time of disclosure or otherwise designated in writing as proprietary by the Disclosing Party within thirty (30) days of disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The original text includes 'Proprietary Information, assembly, component or any technical data or software' under export control, while the changed text omits 'Proprietary Information, assembly, component or any technical data or software'. The contradicted text defines 'Proprietary Information.' By removing these items from the export control clause, the agreement no longer adequately regulates the export of Proprietary Information as defined, creating a direct conflict and ambiguity in enforcement. The change directly contradicts the contradicted text, since the definition of "Proprietary Information" is still present, but the clause that should be controlling it no longer mentions it.
